free_text: Neutron emission, mass distribution and energetics in
The energy of neutron was spread at the target from
14.0 to 14.9 MeV due to a rather large solid angle
subtended by it at the neutron source. The mean
effective neutron energy was estimated to be 14.5 MeV
The uranium target was made in a circular form with
a diameter of 3 cm by electro-spraying a solution of
uranylacetate onto an about 90 ug/cm2 thick nickel
backing. The uranium thickness was determined to be
The U-target was placed between two detectors with the
center of the detectors, the U-target and the N-source
placed in a straight line at an angle of 60 degr. with
respect to the deuteron beam. Neutrons bombarded the
target through the near detector. The distance between
the neutron source and the target was 1.8 cm.
Energies of both fragments and the flight time of one
fragment were measured event by event. About 10000
One of the fragment detectors was a silicon
surface-barrier detector placed at a distance of
0.3 cm from the target. The complementary fragment was
detected by a large area detector located at the end
of a 101.8 cm evacuated flight tube. This detector was
composed of nine identical silicon surface-barrier
detectors arrayed in a mosaic structure and had a
The pre-neutron-emission mass was derived from the
post-neutron-emission masses and energies of both
fragments based on momentum conservation and isotropic
- the effect of the center-of-mass recoil of the
fissioning nucleus due to the impinging neutron
